hook: The arena where Gaul's first Christian martyrs died in 177 AD
facts: Built 19 AD, financed by Caius Iulius Rufus; oldest amphitheatre in Gaul
Federal sanctuary of the cult of Rome and Augustus, sixty Gallic nations
In 177 AD around 48 Christians were martyred here, including the slave Blandina and bishop Pothinus
Only the arena's lower ring survives, in the Jardin des Plantes
legend: Local devotion holds that the column in the arena marks the spot where Blandina was given to the beasts
w: In 177 AD the persecuted Christians of Lugdunum were dragged here to die before the crowd, among them a slave girl, Blandina, who outlasted the beasts and the irons until they finished her by sword. Gaul's oldest arena, financed by sixty Gallic nations. Now a low stone ring in a garden, and a single haunted column.

hook: The great six-storey canut staircase, a traboule of revolt
facts: Built around 1840 in the silk-weavers' quarter, with a monumental open six-floor stairway
Named for the Voraces, a workers' group prominent in the 1848-49 republican risings
Traboule linking Place Colbert, Montée Saint-Sébastien and Rue Imbert-Colomès
Resistance networks used its passages under the Occupation
w: Climb through a courtyard and the whole rear wall becomes staircase, six storeys of bare concrete flights zigzagging into the sky. Built around 1840 for silk weavers and named for the Voraces, the firebrands of the 1848 risings. A plaque calls it a hive where canuts fought for their dignity. Cut through it, as the Resistance did.

hook: A secret courtyard garden encrusted in seashells by a refugee tiler
facts: Built 1957-1983 by Jules Senis Mir (1913-1983), a Spanish anarchist exile and tiler
Columns and arches studded with shells, coral, stones and succulents over 400 square metres
Dedicated to his mother, Rosa Mir Mercader
Hidden behind 83-87 Grande Rue de la Croix-Rousse; open limited hours
w: A tiler who fled Franco's Spain spent the last twenty-five years of his life cladding a hidden courtyard in oyster shells, coral and desert roses, column by column, by hand. He dedicated it to his mother, Rosa Mir. Glimpsed from the street through a gap at number 87. Devotion, made of seashells and grout.

hook: A 24-tonne Alpine boulder that stopped the funicular diggers
facts: Quartzite glacial erratic, carried roughly 175 km from the Alps by Ice Age glaciers
Unearthed in 1861 when funicular tunnelling hit rock too hard to break
Moved a few metres in the 2000s for an underground car park
A Croix-Rousse emblem, with views east to the Alps
legend: Legend says the giant Gargantua flicked it from his shoe; another tale calls it a hard-hearted bailiff's heart, turned to stone
w: Diggers cutting the funicular in 1861 hit something they simply could not break: a 24-tonne lump of quartzite a glacier had shoved here from the Alps. They hauled it up and the plateau adopted it. Legend says Gargantua flicked it from his shoe; another, that a cruel bailiff's heart turned to stone here.

hook: A porticoed 1820s passage now colonised by young designers
facts: Cut by Félix Thiaffait from 1826 across the slope where an Oratorian monastery once stood
One of the few true through-routes climbing the Pentes
Rehabilitated 1997-2001 as the Village des Créateurs, fashion studios
Entered under a portico from Rue René Leynaud
w: Duck under the portico on Rue Leynaud and a steep stone passage opens, a rare honest shortcut up the slope, cut in the 1820s where Oratorian monks once prayed. Long seedy, it was scrubbed up around 2000 and handed to young fashion designers. Monastery to fashion incubator, one of the Pentes' few real ways up.

hook: Working Jacquard looms in the silk weavers' own quarter
facts: At 10-12 rue d'Ivry on the Croix-Rousse plateau
Only place in Lyon to see hand Jacquard looms running, with daily weaving demonstrations
Tells the canuts' story from China to the 1831 and 1834 revolts
Holds fabric samples, shuttles, reels and old machines
w: The canuts wove silk in tall-windowed flats up here so the looms had light; the Jacquard's punched cards let one weaver do the work of many, then put many out of work. Their 1831 revolt flew the black flag: live working, or die fighting. This workshop still clatters, the last place in Lyon to watch a hand Jacquard run.

hook: Europe's largest trompe-l'oeil, a whole painted neighbourhood
facts: Painted 1987 by Cité de la Création, updated 1997 and 2013
Covers a blind facade of around 1,200 square metres, said to be Europe's largest mural
Depicts everyday Croix-Rousse life, stairs, shops and residents
At the corner of Boulevard des Canuts and Rue Denfert-Rochereau
w: A blank gable end painted to swallow you whole: stairs that aren't there, a baker, kids, a slice of Croix-Rousse rendered so convincingly you step into it. Around 1,200 square metres, said to be Europe's biggest trompe-l'oeil. Repainted over the years so the residents quietly age. The wall that pretends to be a street.

hook: The oldest, steepest climb up the silk-workers' hill
facts: Oldest crossing of the hill, on a path used since prehistory
Long the main artery linking Lyon to Bresse, Franche-Comté and Switzerland until 1769
Cradle of the canut weavers; roughly 705 looms counted nearby in 1788
Climbs around 400 metres from Place des Capucins to the Fernand-Rude esplanade
w: Before the carriage roads, every merchant bound for Switzerland sweated up this one cobbled lane, a track already old in prehistory. By 1788 looms crammed its slopes, and in 1831 canuts poured down it toward the city in revolt. The steepest, oldest way up the hill, still gardens, washing lines and breathless calves.

hook: Hand silkscreen printing on velvet, the last of its kind
facts: At 33 rue Romarin, end of the courtyard on the right
Hand-prints panne velvet and silk in the old Lyonnais tradition
Demonstrations of frame printing and velvet flocking for visitors
Sells scarves and squares stamped Made in Lyon
w: At the back of a courtyard off Rue Romarin, someone still drags pigment across silk by hand and burns patterns into velvet, the way Lyon's workshops did before machines took over. Free demonstrations, and squares you can watch being printed. A working survival of the craft that made the city rich, not a museum case.

hook: Lugdunum's Roman theatre, still hosting summer nights
facts: First theatre built around 15 BC, among the oldest in Roman Gaul
Enlarged in the 2nd century to roughly 108 metres, seating about 10,000
Paired with a smaller odeon for around 2,500 just to the south
Hosts the Nuits de Fourvière festival each summer
w: Cut into the Fourvière slope around 15 BC, this stone bowl held ten thousand Lugdunum citizens, with a snug odeon beside it for the highbrow stuff. Lost under the hill for centuries, then dug out. Every summer the Nuits de Fourvière fill its tiers again, an audience sitting where Gallo-Romans sat two thousand years back.

hook: Reassembled Roman tombs of Lugdunum's freedmen elite
facts: Found 1885 at Trion during railway works; five of ten tombs rebuilt stone by stone
Reconstructed on Place Eugène-Wernert, top of the Montée de Choulans
Dating from the 1st century, classified historic monuments in 1905
Best preserved belonged to the sevir Turpio, a freedman turned city notable
w: Railway navvies in 1885 cut straight through a Roman cemetery; rather than bin the tombs, the city dismantled five and rebuilt them block by block on a quiet square. The grandest belonged to Turpio, a freed slave who clawed his way into Lugdunum's elite and meant everyone to know it. Death, advertised in stone.

hook: Lyon's own iron tower, an Eiffel echo crowning the hill
facts: Built 1893-94 for the 1894 Universal and Colonial Exposition
Around 86 metres tall, modelled on the Eiffel Tower's third stage
Sits at 291 metres altitude, making its summit the city's highest point
Once a public belvedere; now a television transmitter, closed to visitors
w: Lyon watched Paris raise its tower and answered with this lattice of iron in 1894, a knockoff of the Eiffel's top third bolted onto the city's highest hill, right behind the basilica. Crowds once climbed it for the view; now it just beams television. Two hundred tonnes of civic envy, perched at the summit.

hook: A Renaissance pink tower hidden up a traboule
facts: At 16 rue du Boeuf, listed historic monument since 1937
Named Tour Rose for its ochre-pink staircase tower
Reached through a courtyard traboule in the Saint-Jean quarter
Henri IV is said to have lodged here in 1600
w: Thread the traboule off Rue du Boeuf and a rose-pink tower rears up in the courtyard, a Renaissance spiral stair of warm ochre stone. They say Henri IV bedded down here in 1600. Long a secret of Vieux Lyon's silk-merchant houses, now wrapped in a food court. Climb to see the pink for yourself.

hook: Army bakery and convent turned riverside arts laboratory
facts: On the Saône at 8 bis quai Saint-Vincent, in a 17th-century convent
Army-run from 1807; baked bread and stored wine, tobacco and coffee until 1991
Called the Military Subsistances until handed to the city in 1995
Now an arts research lab and home to Lyon's Beaux-Arts school
w: Nuns, then quartermasters: this riverside convent spent nearly two centuries baking army bread and hoarding the garrison's wine and tobacco. The soldiers left in the 1990s and dancers, acrobats and art students moved into the vast halls. A creation laboratory on the Saône, smelling faintly still of flour and discipline.

hook: A belvedere of bolted chairs facing Mont Blanc
facts: Opened 2001, a gift from Montreal marking twenty years as Lyon's sister city
At Place de l'Abbé-Larue, around 232 metres up in Saint-Just
Six chairs by Quebecois sculptor Michel Goulet fixed to the ground at odd angles
On clear days Mont Blanc shows on the horizon
w: Montreal gave Lyon this hilltop terrace for twenty years of sisterhood, and a Quebecois sculptor bolted six chairs to it at contradictory angles, each daring you to sit and look. One seat's inscription swears that on a clear day you can spot Mont Blanc, a hundred and fifty kilometres east. A viewpoint about looking.
